gold.rationale: The source material only supports that Yang Zi's studio objected to unauthorized AI face-swapped/deep-synthesized videos and that the related videos were taken down after complaints. The article goes far beyond that record by inventing a platform-backed commercial rollout narrative and using synthetic or polished visuals as if they documented an official release.

atoms: A woman in ancient Chinese-style costume with elaborate golden hair ornaments and a forehead mark.
Large golden calligraphy text reading '花笺记' (Hua Jian Ji) on the left side.
Vertical text next to the title reading '快意恩仇·宿命难逃'.
Top navigation bar with '新剧推荐', '关注', '发现', '推荐', and a search icon.
Right-side interaction icons: a circular profile with '花笺', a heart with '28.6万', a comment bubble with '1.2万', a star with '3.0万', and a share arrow.
Bottom text block: '@花笺记官方', description text, update schedule '每日12:00更新2集', and '热播榜TOP3 · 98.3万人在看'.
A small circular logo with '花笺' and a music note icon at the bottom right.
A screenshot of a Weibo post from '杨紫工作室' (Yang Zi Studio).
Post timestamp: '26-3-20 16:33', location: '北京'.
Main text body discussing unauthorized use of AI face-swap and deep synthesis on Yang Zi's likeness.
Text mentions '委托律师取证' (entrusted lawyers to collect evidence) and '法律途径追究到底' (pursue to the end via legal channels).
Text states '相关视频已下架' (related videos have been taken down).
A blue '+关注' (Follow) button in the top right corner.
A '当事人' (Party Involved) tag next to the studio name.
A screenshot of a video player interface showing a woman in orange historical costume with tears and facial injuries.
Title text on the left: '重生后我成了娘亲的守护神' (After Rebirth, I Became My Mother's Guardian Deity).
Episode indicator 'EP14 逆天改命' in the top right corner.
Right-side interaction icons: a red gift box with '18.99', a star with '3.7万', a comment bubble with '177', a heart with '981', and a share arrow with '639'.
Bottom text: '第14集 | 逆天改命', '热评 | 哭的时候好像杨紫', and '作者声明：内容由AI生成' (Author Statement: Content generated by AI).
A '官方独播' (Official Exclusive Broadcast) badge in the bottom right corner.
A screenshot of a customer service chat interface titled '在线客服' (Online Customer Service).
A system message stating the platform has received feedback and requests submission of complaint materials to 'hongguoju bao@bytedance.com'.
User message: '现在这个短剧搜不到了，是什么情况' (I can't find this short drama now, what's going on?).
Customer service reply: '可能下架了哦' (It might have been taken down).
Customer service reply: '还有其他可以帮到你的吗~' (Is there anything else I can help you with?).
A small thumbnail of the video from image 3 is visible in the top right corner of the chat.
A composite infographic titled 'The Clone Economy' with a woman's face on the left.
Facial landmark mapping overlay on the woman's face with points and lines.
Text listing facial landmarks: 'LEFT EYE', 'RIGHT EYE', 'NOSE', 'MOUTH', 'JAWLINE', 'CHEEKBONE', 'BROW', 'LIP CONTOUR'.
Metrics displayed: 'SIMILARITY SCORE 98.7%' and 'MODEL CONFIDENCE HIGH'.
A 'DEEPFAKE GENERATION' panel on the right showing 'SOURCE FOOTAGE' and 'AI GENERATED' side-by-side comparison.
Waveform visualizations for 'VIDEO WAVEFORM', 'AUDIO WAVEFORM', and 'LIP SYNC ACCURACY 96%'.
A smartphone mockup at the bottom right showing an app interface named 'STARCLONE' with 'EXCLUSIVE SERIES' and 'BEHIND THE SCENES' sections.
Bottom section 'THE AI CLONING PIPELINE' with five steps: 'DATA COLLECTION', 'MODEL TRAINING', 'SIMULATION', 'RENDERING', 'DISTRIBUTION'.
This image is identical to image 4 (04_img_4.jpg).
A screenshot of a customer service chat interface titled '在线客服' (Online Customer Service).
A system message stating the platform has received feedback and requests submission of complaint materials to 'hongguoju bao@bytedance.com'.
User message: '现在这个短剧搜不到了，是什么情况' (I can't find this short drama now, what's going on?).
Customer service reply: '可能下架了哦' (It might have been taken down).
Customer service reply: '还有其他可以帮到你的吗~' (Is there anything else I can help you with?).

visual_indicators.scene_description: A promotional screenshot for a short drama titled '花笺记' (Hua Jian Ji). It features a woman in elaborate ancient Chinese costume with golden hair ornaments. The interface includes navigation tabs, engagement metrics (likes, comments, shares), and text indicating it is a new drama recommendation with 98.3k viewers.

visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image visually presents a polished, official-looking short drama interface, consistent with the post's description of a 'platform-backed production' that appeared legitimate.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A screenshot of a Weibo post from '杨紫工作室' (Yang Zi Studio). The text details a legal objection against unauthorized AI face-swapping and deep synthesis using Yang Zi's likeness. It mentions evidence collection by lawyers and the takedown of related videos.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: 杨紫工作室 | 当事人 | 26-3-20 16:33 | 演员杨紫工作室官博 | 发布于北京 | 针对某作品未经授权，利用AI换脸、深度合成等技术，擅自使用杨紫女士的肖像制作并传播视频内容的行为，严重侵犯杨紫女士合法权益。工作室已委托律师对侵权内容进行取证，并将通过法律途径追究到底！目前相关视频已下架。技术可以创造美好未来，但不该成为侵权的工具。网络不是法外之地，工作室坚决维护艺人权益，绝不姑息任何侵权行为！ | 大人公益 | +关注
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image directly visualizes the official statement mentioned in the post text, confirming the date (March 20) and the specific claims about AI infringement and video takedowns.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A screenshot of a video player showing a dramatic scene from a short drama titled '重生后我成了娘亲的守护神'. The woman in the video has visible tears and facial injuries. The interface shows episode number 14, engagement metrics, and a crucial disclaimer stating 'Content generated by AI'.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A screenshot of a customer service chat log. The user asks why a short drama is no longer searchable. The customer service agent replies that it might have been taken down. A thumbnail of the video from image 3 is visible in the chat.
visual_indicators.ocr_text: 在线客服 | 平台已收到你反馈的情况，为方便平台核实相关问题，请按如下投诉材料标准补正补齐投诉材料并提交至邮箱 hongguoju bao@bytedance.com。平台将在收到你的完整投诉材料后及时核查处理。感谢你的关注与支持！ | 欢迎回来，请继续和在线客服进行咨询 | 现在这个短剧搜不到了，是什么情况 | 可能下架了哦 | 还有其他可以帮到你的吗~
visual_indicators.image_text_alignment_hint: The image provides visual evidence of the platform's response to user complaints, corroborating the post's claim that the videos were removed after feedback.

visual_indicators.scene_description: A complex infographic titled 'The Clone Economy'. It illustrates the process of creating AI clones of celebrities, showing facial mapping, deepfake generation, and a mobile app interface for streaming these clones. It includes technical metrics like similarity scores and lip sync accuracy.

retrieved_evidence.text: 【#擅自使用杨紫肖像的AI短剧已下架#】#曝AI短剧使用杨紫的脸#，对此@杨紫 工作室发声：针对某作品未经授权，利用AI换脸、深度合成等技术，擅自使用杨紫肖像制作并传播视频内容的行为，严重侵犯其合法权益。工作室已委托律师对侵权内容进行取证，并将通过法律途径追究到底！目前相关视频已下架。“技术 ​​​

Yang Zi’s studio, which manages the actress’s commercial and publicity affairs, issued a public statement condemning the unauthorized use of AI face-swapping and so‑called deep synthesis technologies involving her appearance. In the text, highlighted by Chinese entertainment media, the studio argued that these practices had infringed Yang Zi’s lawful rights and personal dignity, stressing that the dispute went beyond ordinary fan-made edits and touched on emerging problems in the regulation of algorithmically produced performers on large short-video platforms.

Yang Zi’s studio issued a statement in recent days objecting to the use of the actress’s likeness in AI face-swapped or other deeply synthesized video content, according to a notice circulated on Chinese social media. The studio said it had discovered online clips in which Yang’s face was digitally overlaid onto unrelated scenes without authorization, and described such practices as damaging to her image. Representatives indicated that they are collecting evidence and may seek to hold relevant producers or distributors accountable through legal channels, while further details of potential actions remain under discussion.

#杨紫工作室回应AI换脸杨紫##红果回应有AI短剧使用杨紫的脸# 今日，#曝AI短剧使用杨紫的脸# 引发广泛关注。媒体就此事向红果平台核实，平台客服表示已收到相关反馈，目前涉事视频已下架处理。
随后@杨紫工作室 发布声明，明确此类AI换脸行为已严重侵犯杨紫合法权益，工作室已委托律师取证，并将通过法 ​​​

Media graphics and expert commentary on the Yang Zi case have depicted a streamlined industrial pipeline in which a celebrity-style portrait can be ingested by an AI face-mapping interface and then deployed across a mobile drama dashboard for serialized micro-dramas. Rights specialists noted that the combination of realistic facial cloning, automated costume generation and app-based distribution allows platforms to repurpose recognizable likenesses into low-cost, AI-driven content at scale, intensifying debates over portrait rights, consent and revenue-sharing in China’s entertainment sector.

Customer-service records cited in recent coverage showed that when Yang Zi’s representatives or fans attempted to locate the disputed micro-drama, platform support staff confirmed that the title could no longer be found in the catalog. These chat transcripts indicated the complaint had been registered but that the content was already unavailable, prompting legal experts and commentators to question how broadly the AI-derived series might have been distributed prior to removal and what internal review procedures the short-video platform had applied.

Industry accounts examining the case of Yang Zi emphasized that the contested fantasy micro-drama was not merely circulating as a casual user creation but appeared within the short-video app’s vertical interface in a format indistinguishable from premium episodic releases. The series layout, episode labeling and promotional banners suggested a standardized, officially curated program featuring a lead character modeled on Yang Zi’s facial features, reinforcing concerns that the platform had integrated an unauthorized digital stand‑in into its conventional content pipeline.

The controversy surrounding the alleged misuse of actress Yang Zi’s image in AI-related video clips has been characterized by her studio as a matter of infringement of lawful rights, according to a statement released this week. The studio emphasized that any unauthorized use of her portrait, name or identity for commercial or widely disseminated content could violate personality and related rights under existing regulations. It called on producers and platforms to strengthen review mechanisms and urged users to refrain from forwarding questionable materials, while saying that relevant legal procedures may be pursued if necessary.

International observers watching developments in China’s AI and entertainment regulation have cited the Yang Zi case as a prominent example of how generative technologies intersect with star-driven media economies. Comparative analyses link the controversy to global debates on deepfake performers, synthetic influencers and digital doubles, arguing that short-video platforms’ experiments with AI micro-dramas will likely shape emerging standards for consent, attribution and monetization involving the digital personas of actors and other public figures.

Technologists involved in AI development note that modern face-swapping and generative video systems can now create highly convincing replicas of public figures such as Yang Zi using relatively small training datasets. In discussions about the short-video platform case, they emphasize that user-friendly interfaces have lowered technical barriers, making it easy for producers and studios to test different celebrity likenesses in micro-dramas, which in turn raises complex questions about accountability when platform branding suggests official endorsement.

Within the broader entertainment ecosystem, Chinese short-video providers have aggressively promoted micro-dramas and vertical series as new revenue drivers, often blending human performances with algorithmically generated backdrops and characters. Analysts observing the Yang Zi dispute suggest that the commercial incentive to create instantly familiar digital leads can encourage the replication of popular actors’ faces, and that without clear contractual frameworks, this dynamic risks normalizing unlicensed face-cloning as a routine production technique.

Commentaries in Chinese entertainment media have pointed out that the available statements from Yang Zi’s studio refer only to unauthorized use of her likeness in specific video works, rather than confirming any broader platform-produced drama franchise, according to recent reports. Analysts noted that references in the dispute focus on individual clips or short videos circulated online, and there has been no official documentation indicating that a full series or branded program involving AI-modified versions of the actress has been formally developed. Observers say further information from platforms would be needed to clarify the scope of the content involved.

In recent reports on China’s booming short-video industry, a major mobile streaming platform was described as promoting a Chinese-style fantasy micro-drama built around a heroine closely resembling actress Yang Zi, dressed in ornate historical costume and shown within a familiar app-style interface. Observers noted that the work was marketed with a glossy, platform-branded release aesthetic typical of official serial content, raising concerns that an advanced algorithmic digital replica of Yang Zi’s likeness had been used without authorization as part of a broader commercial push for face-cloned entertainment characters.

Consumer advocates responding to the Yang Zi incident have highlighted the perspective of ordinary viewers who encounter AI-based micro-dramas within heavily gamified short-video apps. According to recent commentary, the seamless integration of digital replicas into fantasy series, recommendation feeds and branded release pages can make it difficult for audiences to distinguish between authorized collaborations and unlicensed cloning, underscoring calls for clearer on-screen disclosures and stronger penalties for misuse of celebrity likenesses.

Industry observers reviewing public statements related to the Yang Zi image dispute say the material released so far does not indicate that the actress herself has participated in, endorsed, licensed or negotiated any AI-driven project. According to summaries circulated by legal and entertainment commentators this week, the studio’s position centers on opposition to the unauthorized use of her likeness, without mentioning any cooperation frameworks. Analysts suggest that, in the absence of explicit contracts or endorsements, speculation about direct involvement by the performer should be treated with caution.

Legal scholars examining the controversy surrounding Yang Zi and Chinese short-video platforms have pointed to China’s evolving regulatory framework on deep synthesis technologies, including provisions that require prominent labeling of algorithmically produced content and explicit consent for the commercial use of an individual’s likeness. Commentators argue that the incident illustrates gaps between written rules and platform-level enforcement, with the rapid growth of AI micro-dramas challenging existing mechanisms for rights protection and complaint resolution.

Several Chinese entertainment news outlets and popular social media accounts reported this week that the video materials at the center of the recent dispute involving actress Yang Zi had been removed from major platforms. According to these accounts, links shared by users now display error messages or are no longer publicly accessible, suggesting that platform operators or content uploaders may have taken them down. Commentators noted that the specific timing and the party initiating the removal have not been clearly disclosed, and follow-up measures by regulators or rights holders remain to be seen.

text_analysis.alignment_reasoning: The post accurately reports the core event (Yang Zi's studio objection and takedown) but significantly distorts the narrative by framing it as a confirmed 'platform-supported' commercial rollout of a specific AI series, whereas evidence indicates the scope remains unconfirmed and likely involves unauthorized user uploads.
text_analysis.t2_evidence: Post claims the incident resembles a 'failed trial run in platform-supported synthetic entertainment' and describes it as a 'structured content rollout' (news_090_5 states 'no official documentation indicating that a full series... has been formally developed' and news_090_1 implies clips were 'circulated online' without confirming platform production).
t2_problematic_subclaims: The incident is described as a 'platform-supported synthetic entertainment' trial run and a 'structured content rollout' rather than unauthorized user uploads.
The post frames the event as a 'commercial test balloon' and 'platform-backed production' rather than an isolated infringement case.
The narrative implies a deliberate 'industrial input' strategy by the platform rather than a reactive takedown of rogue content.

image_analysis.global_notes: All images are consistent with the post's narrative about the Yang Zi AI micro-drama controversy. Images 1, 3, 4, and 6 are synthetic or heavily edited, while images 2 and 5 are plain screenshots of official communications.

judge.level3_rationale: 该帖子将杨紫工作室的维权事件歪曲为平台支持的商业测试，这与证据表明这是未经授权的具体视频作品使用相矛盾，构成了叙事扭曲和语境误导。
judge.reasoning_trace: The post claims the incident was a 'platform-supported commercial rollout' and 'structured content rollout', but evidence [src_090_1] and [ctx_090_07] clarify it was unauthorized use in specific video works without official documentation of a franchise. This constitutes T2 Distortion (misrepresenting the nature of the event) and T3 Misleading Context (framing it as a platform experiment). The images are real screenshots that support the visual description but do not validate the post's false narrative about the platform's intent.
judge.core_claim: The Yang Zi incident was a 'failed trial run' of a platform-supported, commercial AI micro-drama rollout that was quietly withdrawn after backlash.
key_subclaims: The disputed content was a 'platform-backed production' or 'structured content rollout' rather than unauthorized user uploads.
The incident represents a 'commercial test balloon' or 'semi-formal AI release' by a major platform.
The platform promoted the content with polished promotional imagery and recommendation placement to lend it legitimacy.
The specific micro-drama titled 'Reborn, I Became My Mother's Guardian Deity' was the primary vehicle for this commercial test.
The images circulating were 'promotional stills' and 'interface screenshots' that created the 'commercial aura' of a platform-backed production.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The disputed content was a 'platform-backed production' or 'structured content rollout' rather than unauthorized user uploads.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence states the studio objected to unauthorized use of her likeness in specific video works, with no official documentation indicating a full series or branded program was formally developed.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The incident represents a 'commercial test balloon' or 'semi-formal AI release' by a major platform.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence clarifies that statements refer only to unauthorized use in specific video works, not a broader platform-produced franchise or commercial test.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The platform promoted the content with polished promotional imagery and recommendation placement to lend it legitimacy.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence suggests the work appeared within the app's vertical interface in a format indistinguishable from premium releases, but this is described as an appearance, not necessarily a deliberate promotional campaign by the platform.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The specific micro-drama titled 'Reborn, I Became My Mother's Guardian Deity' was the primary vehicle for this commercial test.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence confirms the contested fantasy micro-drama appeared within the short-video app's vertical interface.

subclaim_findings.subclaim: The images circulating were 'promotional stills' and 'interface screenshots' that created the 'commercial aura' of a platform-backed production.
subclaim_findings.note: Evidence notes the series layout and promotional banners suggested a standardized, officially curated program.

judge.verdict_reasoning: The post's core claim that the incident was a 'platform-supported commercial rollout' is contradicted by evidence stating it was unauthorized use in specific video works without official documentation of a franchise. The post distorts the narrative by framing it as a 'structured content rollout' and 'commercial test balloon' (T2). It also misleads the context by presenting the incident as a deliberate platform experiment rather than an infringement case (T3). The images are real screenshots that support the visual description but do not validate the post's false narrative about the platform's intent.
